I typically will go up and dissect out the hiatus first, preserving, and I think the biggest thing that we've learned over the years, if you're going to reduce the likelihood for hernia, you want to preserve the hiatus as much as you can.
And I think one of the problems with doing, Dan Dunn is another person who does a trans-hiatal approach,
That is, you get higher and higher in the mediastinum, the arms will stretch the hilum and cause dysfunction.

And basically, my problem with robotic esophagectomy is that, and as Sir wrote in his paper, this is the only way that you have a totally robotic esophagectomy, where everything is done intracorporeally, everything is done with the robot.
Because if you do an EEA stapler,
Basically what happens is that you do 90% of the operation robotic, and then you're converted to a thoracoscopic anastomotic technique.
Yes, you have the robotic camera in there, but you don't have the robotic instrument doing the anastomosis.
You're putting the stapler in, you have to manipulate, and you put the anvil in, whatever.
Basically, I was like, all right, if I'm going to do robotic subjectomy after a two-year journey and, you know, a number of complications, I said I've got to do this robotically this way.
